content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
March22,
1993
William F.
Northrop
Executive Vice President
Independent Oil Producers’
Agency
1228
N Street,
Ste.
26
Sacramento,
CA
95814
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-93-088
Dear Mr.
Northrop:
You have requested advice on behalf of the Independent Oil
Producers’
Agency
(“IOPA”)
concerning the gift provisions of the
Political Reform Act
(the hIAct).:h/
QUESTION
Would a solid plastic container with two samples of crude oil
sealed in clear material along with the
IOPA logo be considered
a
gift if given to legislative and state agency officials or would
the container be considered informational material?
CONCLUSION
The container would be deemed a gift to the legislative and
agency officials who receive it.
FACTS
Your letter provides the following information:
We searched for a graphic manner which would
clearly demonstrate the unique properties of California-
produced heavy crude oil.
At first we used small glass
jars and compared our oil to the oil produced in other
parts of the nation.
after several of these jars broke,
1J
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
et
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428
J Street. Suite 8(K)
•
P.O. Box 807
•
Stcramento, C:\
)Sl2
•
I
2-cc0


William F.
Northrop
Our File No.
A—93-088
Page
2
we decided that a plastic container of some sort would
have to be substituted or we would have to abandon the
practice altogether.
After several tries we found a plastics
manufacturer who would make a “solid” container with two
samples of crude oil sealed in a clear material with our
logo.
The two sanples,
which vary tremendously in
viscosity, are used to demonstrate the challenges
inherent in the production of California’s crude ci]..
We have found that schools and others interested in
California’s energy industry find these samples to be a
worthwhile tool with which to demonstrate the unique
qualities of California heavy crude oil.
From time to time legislative issues arise which
require knowledge of the difference between physical
properties of California crude oil and oil produced in
other regions.
This knowledge is necessary to make
informed legislative decisions.
At such times it would
be meaningful for legislators to have our sample at hand
for reference and demonstration purposes.
We also think
this visual aid would be helpful in discussions with
staff people at various state agencies concerning the
problems associated with the extraction of California
heavy crude and petroleum operations distinctive to
California.
Sometime prior to receipt of your letter,
a sample of the
container was delivered to this office for viewing and was later
returned to IOPA.
The container is clear,
solid plastic.
On one
side is a small sample of clear liquid accompanied by the word
“light.”
On the other side is a small sample of a thick,
dark
liquid accompanied by the word “heavy.”
In the center is a gold-
colored reproductcn of IOPA’s logo.
ANALYSIS
The Act requires disclosure of and places limitations on the
making of gifts tc public officials.
(Sections 86203,
37207,
89504—89505.)
Section 82028 defines the term “gift”
and provides:
(a)
“Gift” means,
except as provided in
subdivision
(b),
any payment to the extent that
consideration of equal or greater value is not
received.
(b)
The term “gift” does not include:
(1)
Informational material such as books, reports,
pamphlets,
calendars,
or periodicals....
The term “informational material” has been further defined
by
Regulation 18942.1 to mean:
• any item which serves primarily to convey information
and which is provided to an official for the purpose of


William F.
Northrop
Our File
No.
A—93—088
Page
3
assisting him or her in the performance of his or her
official duties.
Informational material may include:
(a)
Books, reports,
pamphlets,
calendars,
periodicals, videotapes,
or free admission to
informational conferences or seminars.
(b)
Scale models, pictorial representations,
maps,
and other such items,
provided that where the item has
a
fair market value in excess cf $250,
the burden shall be
on the official to demonstrate Lhat the item is
informational material.
Having viewed the plastic container,
we feel that the
container is
a gift rather than informational material.
It
supplies minimal information,
and appears to be more decorative
than informational in nature.
Therefore,
if the containers are
provided to legislative or state agency officials,
they will
be
deemed gifts to those officials.
If you have questions concerning this letter, please contact
me at
(916)
322—5662.
Sincerely,
Jeff Marschner
C3ricral Counsel
OAL
L--)
By:
Carla Wardlow
Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division

a

sealed in clear material along with the
IOPA logo be considered
a

CONCLUSION: The container would be deemed a gift to the legislative and

CONCLUSION The container would be deemed a gift to the legislative and agency officials who receive it.
